dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 6 responding for breathing difficulty.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMS provider with Rescue Unit 6. What made you call 911 today?
Difficulty breathing.
How are you doing today?
A bit better, I think.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event (A&OX4). Your GCS is 15.
You opened your eyes spontaneously and are following objects and responding appropriately.
Your verbal responses are limited due to shortness of breath, but you are responsive to questions.
You are obeying commands and moving appropriately with stimulation.
Your airway is clear and patent. I don't see any injuries to your airway, nose, or throat.
Your breathing is labored, and I notice you are using accessory muscles.
You're breathing at 12 breaths per minute, which is shallow. I'll check your oxygen saturation.
I notice an increased effort with breathing, and you are in a tripod position. There is no cyanosis observed.
I've listened to your lungs and found both sides to be absent.
Your pulse is elevated at 132 beats per minute, and it's strong.
Bilateral radial pulses are present and strong.
Your skin is warm and diaphoretic-sweaty to touch.
Initial blood pressure is currently unobtainable.
No signs of active bleeding on your body.
From the call, you have a documented history of COPD. Is that correct?
When did your shortness of breath start today?
Started yesterday.
Any coughing, fever, chills, or recent exposure to someone with possible illness such as COVID-19?
No. No chest pain either.
You are a 64-year-old female, and your weight is 91 kg.
No signs of trauma or injury to your head or neck.
No signs of tracheal deviation or jugular venous distension.
No visible markings, rashes, or scars on my initial inspection.
We are using a CPAP machine to assist your breathing. Initial SpO2 was 73%; it has improved to 90%.
A 4-lead ECG is being used to monitor your heart rhythm.
Your lung sounds remain absent bilaterally.
We've switched your nebulizer to portable oxygen, and SpO2 improved to 90%.
We placed you on CPAP with an inline nebulizer and sequentially increased the PEEP to 10 cmH2O.
You received 5 mg of albuterol via nebulizer.
A 10 mg dose of dexamethasone was administered intramuscularly.
We attempted an IV start and succeeded in placing a 20G line in your right forearm.
Magnesium sulfate was considered but could not be administered due to time constraints before transport.
Since we started CPAP with elevated PEEP, your breathing has stabilized and improved.
Updated vitals at 17:26 are: pulse 134, respirations 10, SpO2 at 99%, and blood pressure of 211/122.
Latest vitals at 17:31-pulse 141, respirations 12, SpO2 99%, and blood pressure of 187/159.
A 12-lead EKG was performed and interpreted as sinus rhythm. No ectopy or ST elevation found.
Your skin doesn't show any scars, rashes, bruising, or bite marks.
I do not see any signs of jugular venous distension on your neck.
Your extremities show no signs of deformity, and your range of motion is intact.
Your abdomen is soft, not distended, and normal in sound.
No particular issues with urination frequency or urgency were observed.
Your movement and sensation in all extremities remain intact.
I found no DCAP-BTLS impairments on the body check.
Your range of motion is intact in all four extremities.
Your pelvis was assessed and found to be stable with no crepitus or instability.
You're being transported to VCU Health Systems following protocol for a COPD exacerbation.
You were moved out using a stair chair and secured with seat belts.
You were able to stand and pivot with assistance to the stretcher.
During transport, you were monitored continuously and placed safely in the ambulance.
A full care report has been given to VCU.
This is Jordan Deebo, NRP, from Rescue Unit 6. Andrew Hartung, NRP assisted during transport.
Initial blood pressure readings were not measurable (0/0) on scene.
Glucose levels were not obtainable throughout the assessment.
You were found in a tripod position, indicating significant breathing difficulty.
No instability or pain in your pelvis was noted during examination.
No edema or swelling was noted in either extremity.
ETCO2 levels were documented but not available before transport.
Your pain level was noted as 0 throughout the care process.
